title: Planning permission for off-grid installations in England
subtitle: What permitted development covers and what requires an application
excerpt: Most small-scale off-grid energy and water installations in England are covered by permitted development rights and do not need a planning application. The exceptions are listed buildings, conservation areas, and installations that exceed the permitted size thresholds.
sourceNotes: Planning Portal permitted development guidance for householders (England, 2024). DESNZ guidance on Class G permitted development for microgeneration. Historic England guidance on renewable energy in the historic environment. The Town and Country Planning (General Permitted Development) (England) Order 2015 and amendments.

glossaryTerms.definition: Works that are automatically granted planning permission by Parliament under the Town and Country Planning (General Permitted Development) Order, without requiring a formal application. Permitted development rights can be removed or restricted for listed buildings, conservation areas, and some types of controlled land.

content.text: Planning rules differ in Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Check the relevant national planning portal for rules outside England.

rights cover many energy and water installations without a planning application. The key legislation is the Town and Country Planning (General Permitted Development) (England) Order 2015, Class G (microgeneration) and Class H (solar panels). Building regulations are separate from planning and apply regardless of whether planning permission is needed.

Permitted development under Class H if: the installation does not protrude more than 200 mm beyond the roof plane, it is not on a listed building or in a conservation area, and panels are removed when no longer needed. On a flat roof, panels may be angled more steeply but must not rise above the roof ridge.
Ground-mounted solar panels in a garden

Permitted development if: the panel array covers no more than 9 m2 (measured as the surface area, not the footprint), is no taller than 4 m, and is not installed within 5 m of the property boundary. Only one ground-mounted array is allowed per property under permitted development.

A battery storage system fitted with or after a solar installation is permitted development if: it is on a building, no greater than 1 m3 in volume, and not on the front elevation facing a highway. A battery in a shed or garage is covered. A large external cabinet visible from the street needs a permitted development check.

A garden micro wind turbine is permitted development if: hub height is no more than 11.1 m, rotor diameter no more than 3.1 m, the turbine is at least the hub height from the boundary, and noise does not exceed 45 dB(A) at any neighbouring property. In practice, most urban and suburban sites would not get planning permission due to turbulence from nearby buildings.

Water butts, underground rainwater tanks, and greywater systems are not covered by planning law unless they require significant excavation or a visible above-ground structure. A below-ground tank under a garden does not need planning permission. Building regulations do apply to any mains water connection or plumbing involved.
Listed buildings: permitted development rights do not apply. All external changes require listed building consent and often full planning permission.
Conservation areas: roof panels on the main or side elevation facing a highway need a prior approval application, even if they would normally be permitted development.
Article 4 directions: some local authorities have removed permitted development rights in certain areas. Check with the local planning authority if unsure.
New-build properties: some have a planning condition that removes householder permitted development rights. Check the title register or contact the local planning authority.
